ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Thai-style omelette (Khai Jiao); closest parent is hen's egg fried preparation A00LC.

preparation_method: beaten eggs cooked in hot oil

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Thai omelette (Khai Jiao) is one omelette made from approximately two eggs, weighing around 120 g after frying in oil.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, whole, cooked, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01129); adapted for Thai-style deep-fried omelette with fish sauce using FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Khai Jiao
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy fried egg values used as the primary macro base. Thai-style Khai Jiao typically uses more oil than a Western omelette, resulting in higher fat content. Values adjusted upward for fat and energy consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data for deep-fried egg dishes. Sodium elevated to reflect fish sauce addition typical in Thai preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; egg-based dishes reference; omelette analogous estimate from whole egg GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style omelette (Khai Jiao). GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on whole cooked egg GI values (approximately 45–50) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 120 g serving calculated as (48 × 1.2 g carbohydrate × 1.2) / 100 = approximately 0.7, confirming negligible glycemic impact. gi_category set to low.

hypertension.guidance: Thai omelette prepared with fish sauce contributes approximately 504 mg sodium per 120 g serving; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or substitute with low-sodium soy sauce to keep daily sodium within the 1500–2000 mg target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a GI of approximately 48, Thai omelette has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and oil quantity is moderated.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content and frying oil quantity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 444 mg dietary cholesterol and elevated total fat from deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider pan-frying with minimal oil to reduce saturated fat and total caloric load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai omelette, known as Khai Jiao (ไข่เจียว), is a classic Thai egg dish made by beating whole eggs with fish sauce and white pepper, then deep-frying or pan-frying in a generous amount of hot vegetable oil until the exterior is golden and slightly crispy while the interior remains soft and custardy. Unlike Western omelettes, Khai Jiao is typically cooked in more oil, giving it a characteristic puffed, lacy-edged appearance and a richer flavour. It is a staple quick-meal item across Thailand and is commonly served over steamed jasmine rice with a drizzle of fish sauce or sriracha. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 13.6 g per 100 g) and fat (approximately 17.2 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (approximately 1.2 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The glycemic index is low (approximately 48), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin A, selenium, and phosphorus. In Singapore, Thai omelette appears on menus at Thai restaurants and food courts, often listed as a side dish or quick-bite option.
common_user_queries: ไข่เจียว nutrition

white pepper: พริกไทยขาว

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for Thai omelette (Khai Jiao) was available. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried egg data with upward fat adjustment consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional deep-fried egg dish data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database whole-egg values. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; eggs contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA). 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; eggs contain no vitamin C (naturally zero). 3. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g reflects minor trans fats naturally present in egg yolk lipids, not industrial trans fats. 4. Sodium of 420 mg per 100 g reflects typical fish sauce addition in Thai preparation; a plain egg omelette without fish sauce would have approximately 142 mg sodium per 100 g. 5. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Thai, Salad, BBQ, Fried Rice Vermicelli' — these appear to be restaurant-level tags for The Upper House - Funan and do not reflect the food itself; cuisine is correctly classified as Thai. 6. food_group retained as 'Dairy and eggs' from input, consistent with egg-based classification. 7. GI source is analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Khai Jiao was not located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all populated from USDA SR Legacy fried egg data (NDB 01129) and are not -1 sentinel values. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record.
